name: Pinot Noir
typical_style: Light-medium body, high acidity, low-medium tannin, high perfume. Extraordinary transparency to terroir.

name: Cabernet Sauvignon
typical_style: Full body, high tannin, high acidity. Demands aging. The world's most widely planted noble red variety.

name: Merlot
typical_style: Medium-full body, medium tannin, medium-high acidity. Softer and fleshier than Cabernet Sauvignon. Key blending partner.

name: Chardonnay
typical_style: Neutral variety that reflects winemaking and terroir. Can range from lean, mineral, and unoaked (Chablis) to rich, creamy, and oaky (Napa, Meursault).

name: Riesling
typical_style: High acidity across all sweetness levels, from bone-dry to lusciously sweet. Ages magnificently. The world's most transparent variety to soil type.

name: Sauvignon Blanc
typical_style: High acidity, medium body, dry. Two styles: Loire (mineral, herbaceous) vs New World (tropical, exuberant). Best without oak or with brief neutral oak.

name: Syrah
typical_style: Full body, high tannin, medium-high acidity. Northern Rhône (Syrah): precise, peppery, garrigue. Australian Shiraz: rich, chocolate-forward, warm-climate amplitude.

name: Grenache
typical_style: Medium-full body, low-medium tannin, low acidity, high alcohol. Oxidizes rapidly. Foundational Châteauneuf-du-Pape grape. Ages magnificently in Priorat and old-vine Barossa.

name: Tempranillo
typical_style: Medium-full body, medium-high tannin, medium acidity. Spain's most versatile red — can produce everything from young, fruity wines to grand reserve-aged classics.

name: Sangiovese
typical_style: High acidity, firm tannin, medium-full body. The backbone of Chianti, Brunello, and Vino Nobile. Extraordinary food versatility. Ages exceptionally well.

name: Nebbiolo
typical_style: Light ruby color paradoxically encasing enormous tannin and acidity. Requires extended aging — 15 to 30 years for Barolo's finest expressions. 'Tar and roses' is the classic shorthand.

name: Pinot Gris
typical_style: Two dominant styles: Alsatian (rich, full, slightly off-dry, aging potential) vs Italian Pinot Grigio (crisp, light, high-volume commercial). The best Alsatian examples are world-class.

name: Gewürztraminer
typical_style: Unmistakable variety: deeply aromatic, low acidity, high alcohol, rich texture. Dry versions pair with spicy food; Vendange Tardive versions achieve extraordinary complexity.

name: Viognier
typical_style: Rich, full-bodied, low acidity, high perfume. The vine of Condrieu and Château-Grillet. Co-fermented with Syrah in Côte-Rôtie to add perfume and stabilize color. Best drunk young.

name: Chenin Blanc
typical_style: Extraordinary versatility: bone-dry mineral (Savennières) through off-dry (Vouvray demi-sec) to dessert (Coteaux du Layon Sélection). Legendary aging potential. South Africa produces outstanding modern expressions.

name: Sémillon
typical_style: Low acidity, waxy texture, susceptible to noble rot. In Sauternes the key variety. Hunter Valley Sémillon is a benchmark for dry aging: starting austere and lemon-sharp, developing into complex, toasty, honeyed wines over 10–20 years.

name: Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains
typical_style: The finest Muscat variety — the only one that smells and tastes of fresh grapes. Made dry in Alsace; as Asti Spumante (DOCG) or Moscato d'Asti (DOCG) in Piedmont; as Vin Doux Naturel in southern France.

name: Zinfandel
typical_style: California's adopted signature red. Full body, medium-high tannin, high alcohol. Old-vine Zinfandel from Sonoma and Paso Robles produces concentrated, complex wines. Italian Primitivo is drier and earthier.

name: Barbera
typical_style: High acidity, low tannin, full body. Piedmont's everyday workhorse variety that in top sites (Scarrone, Vigna Vecchia) produces genuinely impressive wine. Often oak-aged to add structure.

name: Dolcetto
typical_style: Piedmont's early-drinking, food-friendly red. Low acidity, medium-full body, firm but fine tannin, bitter almond finish. At its best in Dolcetto di Dogliani. Drink within 5 years of vintage.

name: Montepulciano
typical_style: Medium-full body, high acidity, medium-high tannin. Not to be confused with Vino Nobile di Montepulciano (Sangiovese). Montepulciano d'Abruzzo is one of Italy's great value reds; Cerasuolo d'Abruzzo is a compelling rosé.

name: Vermentino
typical_style: Crisp, dry, medium body, medium-high acidity. Sardinia's signature white — Vermentino di Gallura DOCG is the finest expression. Also grown in Provence (as Rolle) where it blends into white Bandol and Cassis.

name: Fiano
typical_style: Medium body, medium-high acidity, distinctive smoky, nutty character. One of southern Italy's finest white varieties. Fiano di Avellino DOCG ages well — 5–10 years develops toasty, honeyed complexity.

name: Greco di Tufo
typical_style: Full body, high acidity, mineral-driven. Greco di Tufo DOCG from the volcanic tufa soils around Avellino produces some of Campania's most age-worthy whites. Distinctive smoky-mineral backbone.

name: Aglianico
typical_style: The Barolo of the South. High tannin, high acidity, full body. Taurasi DOCG and Aglianico del Vulture DOCG require significant aging — 10–20 years for top expressions. Volcanic soils add an incomparable mineral backbone.

name: Touriga Nacional
typical_style: Intense, concentrated, high tannin. Portugal's finest indigenous red variety — the backbone of the greatest Vintage Ports and a standout in unfortified Douro reds. Small berries and tiny yields produce extraordinary concentration.

name: Garnacha Blanca
typical_style: Full body, low acidity, medium-high alcohol. Key blending grape in Châteauneuf-du-Pape blanc and Southern Rhône whites. At its finest in old-vine Roussillon (Grenache Blanc VdN) and cool-climate Spanish sites.

name: Albariño
typical_style: High acidity, medium body, dry, aromatic. Spain's most celebrated white variety from the rain-drenched Atlantic coast of Galicia. Also grown as Alvarinho in Portugal's Vinho Verde. Best drunk young and fresh.

name: Verdejo
typical_style: Medium-high acidity, medium body, distinctly bitter herbal finish. Rueda's signature white variety. Often blended with Sauvignon Blanc; the finest mono-varietal versions age surprisingly well.

name: Palomino
typical_style: Neutral, low-acid variety that is ideal for biological and oxidative aging. The foundation of all dry Sherry styles: Fino, Manzanilla, Amontillado, Oloroso. On its own without aging it is undistinguished.

name: Pedro Ximénez
typical_style: Used almost exclusively for the darkest, sweetest Sherry style: Pedro Ximénez Sherry. Grapes are sun-dried (pasificación) to extreme sugar concentrations. Viscous, almost syrupy, intensely sweet.

name: Grüner Veltliner
typical_style: Austria's signature white. Ranges from light, peppery, food-friendly Federspiel to powerful, mineral Smaragd. The white pepper note is diagnostic. Excellent aging potential in top Smaragd examples.

name: Blaufränkisch
typical_style: Medium-full body, high acidity, medium-high tannin. Austria's finest red variety — shows remarkable terroir expression in Burgenland's different sub-regions. Ages beautifully for 10–20 years.

name: Zweigelt
typical_style: Austria's most widely planted red — a crossing of Blaufränkisch and St. Laurent. Softer, more approachable than Blaufränkisch. Best examples from Neusiedlersee and Kremstal.

name: Malbec
typical_style: Argentina transformed Malbec from Bordeaux blending grape into a world-class varietal. Full body, plush tannin, high alcohol. Mendoza's high altitude Malbec (1000–1500m) is the modern benchmark. Cahors in France produces a leaner, earthier, more tannic style.

name: Carménère
typical_style: Chile's signature red variety, brought from Bordeaux pre-phylloxera and long confused with Merlot. At its best: full body, silky tannin, complex green herb and dark fruit interplay. Misidentified as Merlot in Chile for decades.

name: Tannat
typical_style: Extraordinary tannin levels — one of the world's most tannic varieties. Madiran traditionally required micro-oxygenation or Cabernet blending to soften. Uruguay adopted Tannat as a national variety, producing more approachable expressions.

name: Mourvèdre
typical_style: Late-ripening, heat-demanding variety. The backbone of Bandol (Provence). Dark, powerful, gamey, with high tannin. As Monastrell in Spain (Jumilla, Yecla) it produces dense, concentrated reds. Co-blended in Châteauneuf-du-Pape.

name: Roussanne
typical_style: The finer, more delicate Rhône white variety (compared to Marsanne). Full body, medium-high acidity, excellent aging potential. Blended with Marsanne in Crozes-Hermitage blanc, Hermitage blanc, and St-Péray.

name: Marsanne
typical_style: Full body, low acidity, broad texture. Matures slowly — awkward at 5–10 years but stunning at 15–25. The dominant variety in Hermitage blanc. Also successful in Australia (Tahbilk Marsanne is a benchmark for longevity).

name: Melon de Bourgogne
typical_style: Neutral, high acid variety that achieves its best expression through extended lees aging (sur lie). Muscadet Sèvre et Maine sur Lie can develop extraordinary complexity. Criminally underrated and underpurchased.

name: Gamay
typical_style: Light body, high acidity, low tannin, red fruit driven. The grape of Beaujolais. Ten crus of Beaujolais (Morgon, Moulin-à-Vent, Fleurie, etc.) produce serious, age-worthy wines from granite soils. Carbonic maceration produces the lighter, banana-fruity Nouveau style.

name: Trousseau
typical_style: Light-medium body, high acidity, medium tannin. A specialty of the Jura — Trousseau produces perfumed, mineral reds from specific clay-limestone soils. Also grown as Bastardo in Portugal's Douro and Dão.

name: Poulsard
typical_style: Palest red wine grape in France — often approaching a deep rosé in color due to thin skins. Low tannin, high acidity, delicate. The Jura's most fragile and enchanting red variety. Must be tried from Overnoy, Ganevat, or Bornard.

name: Petit Verdot
typical_style: Dense, inky, high tannin, high acidity, intense color. Used in small percentages in Bordeaux blends for structure, color, and longevity. Rarely bottled as a varietal in Bordeaux; interesting solo expressions from Napa Valley and Australia.

name: Cabernet Franc
typical_style: Medium body, medium-high acidity, medium tannin. More aromatic and lighter than Cabernet Sauvignon. The king of the Loire (Chinon, Saumur-Champigny) and Cheval Blanc's foundation. Increasingly bottled as a varietal in Napa and Ontario.

name: Cinsaut
typical_style: Light-medium body, high acidity, low tannin. A blending variety in southern Rhône and Provence rosé. Old-vine Cinsaut from South Africa and Lebanon produces surprisingly complex, perfumed wines. One parent of Pinotage (with Pinot Noir).

name: Carignan
typical_style: High acidity, high tannin, full body. Once the workhouse of Languedoc — now recognized as a variety producing extraordinary old-vine wines from schist slopes in Priorat (as Samsó), Roussillon, and Sardinia (as Carignano).

name: Grenache Blanc
typical_style: Full body, low acidity, high alcohol potential. Blending partner in white Châteauneuf-du-Pape and Southern Rhône whites. Old-vine expressions from Roussillon produce complex, mineral whites.

name: Picpoul
typical_style: High acidity, light body, bone dry, mineral. Picpoul de Pinet is the classic expression — a versatile, fresh white from Languedoc. Named 'lip stinger' for its acidity. Best with oysters and seafood.

name: Bourboulenc
typical_style: Light-medium body, high acidity, low alcohol. A blending variety in white Châteauneuf-du-Pape and Languedoc whites, contributing freshness and structure. Rarely bottled as a varietal.

name: Ugni Blanc
typical_style: High acidity, neutral flavor profile, light body. The world's most planted white grape — overwhelmingly grown for Cognac and Armagnac distillation (as St-Émilion du Cognac and Folle Blanche). As Trebbiano d'Abruzzo, legendary old-vine bottlings from Valentini defy the variety's reputation.

name: Counoise
typical_style: Light-medium body, high acidity, low tannin. A minor blending variety permitted in Châteauneuf-du-Pape. Used by Château Rayas and Château Beaucastel for its freshness and aromatic lift.

name: Clairette
typical_style: Medium body, medium acidity, oxidizes rapidly if not handled carefully. A blending grape in white Châteauneuf-du-Pape and Languedoc whites. Clairette de Die AOC produces a delicate, Muscat-scented traditional-method sparkling wine.

name: Piquepoul Noir
typical_style: Light-medium body, high acidity. The red version of Picpoul — extremely rare today. A blending grape in some Languedoc reds. Almost no varietal bottlings exist commercially.
